region_of_origin: China (Beijing origin); widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for zhajiangmian; A0FHR (cooked noodles) is the closest defensible base term. Sauce and meat components are not captured in the code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve bowl of fried sauce noodles in Singapore weighs approximately 450 g, consistent with typical hawker and restaurant portions.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Health Promotion Board — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo); composite estimate for zhajiangmian cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy wheat noodle and ground pork entries
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central entry exists for zhajiangmian as a complete dish. HPB Foodo was consulted as the primary approved source for Singapore-context noodle dishes; no exact zhajiangmian entry was found. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (GrabFood menu listing, confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced for plausibility against HPB Foodo noodle dish entries and USDA SR Legacy wheat noodle (FDC ID 20109) and ground pork cooked (FDC ID 167903) entries. Input values are internally consistent with a wheat-noodle-and-meat-sauce dish at approximately 680 kcal per 450g bowl. Values retained with medium confidence and documented accordingly. GrabFood is used only as a cross-reference input; HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central are the approved source anchors.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Noodles, wheat-based, boiled, analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for zhajiangmian. GI estimated at 47 based on boiled wheat noodles (GI ~47, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Boiled wheat noodles consistently fall in the GI range 47–52 in the Sydney GI Database; the lower bound of 47 is used here as the fermented bean paste sauce and protein content (6.67 g/100g) modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response relative to plain noodles. GI 47 is classified as low (GI < 55). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 75.0) / 100 = 35.3 (carbohydrate per 450g serving = 16.67 × 4.5 = 75.0g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g; a 450g bowl delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg — primarily from fermented soybean paste and soy sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200g and request reduced-paste preparation; substituting low-sodium soy sauce and increasing shredded vegetable toppings (cucumber, carrot) can reduce sodium load and improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles with GI ~47 (low) but low dietary fibre (1.11 g/100g) produce a high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~35.3), promoting postprandial glucose elevation at typical bowl portions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce portion size, substitute refined wheat noodles with wholemeal or soba noodles to increase fibre and further lower GI, and add non-starchy vegetable toppings to slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.22 g/100g from minced pork and cooking oil, combined with 400 mg/100g sodium and 15.56 mg/100g cholesterol, presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile per serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit consumption to small portions (150–200g), substitute minced pork with lean chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat, and use minimal oil and low-sodium fermented paste to lower the combined sodium and saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced pork is a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; combined with refined carbohydrates and high sodium, the dish has a net pro-inflammatory profile.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute minced pork with minced chicken or tofu, add anti-inflammatory garnishes such as ginger or sesame seeds, and reduce fermented paste quantity to lower both sodium and inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Sauce Noodles (炸酱面, Zhajiangmian) is a classic Chinese noodle dish originating from Beijing and widely popular across China and in Chinese communities throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The dish consists of boiled wheat noodles topped with a rich, savoury stir-fried sauce made from minced pork (or beef), fermented soybean paste (doubanjiang or tianmianjiang), soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and cooking oil. It is typically garnished with shredded cucumber, blanched bean sprouts, and spring onion. The texture contrasts soft, chewy noodles with a thick, umami-laden meat sauce. Nutritionally, a 450g bowl provides approximately 680 kcal, 30g protein, 28g fat, and 75g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1800 mg per bowl) due to fermented paste and soy sauce, making it a concern for hypertension management. The refined wheat noodle base has a low glycaemic index (~47) but delivers a high glycaemic load per serving (~35.3) due to large portion size, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Saturated fat from pork and cooking oil contributes moderate cardiovascular risk. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation adjustments. It contains gluten (wheat) and soy allergens.

Can diabetics eat fried sauce noodles?
